title: Tecomella undulata
intro: Tecomella undulata, commonly known as the 'Girivalli', is a large, deciduous tree native to the Western Ghats of India. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Bioactive constituents of Tecomella undulata include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Tecomella undulata include decoctions, infusions, and powders, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including digestive issues and respiratory problems.
taxonomy: Tecomella undulata, also known as the ghost gum or murray gum, is a flowering plant native to Australia. It is classified within the family Bignonaceae. This plant is further categorized into the genus Tecomella, which contains only one species. The classification of Tecomella undulata reflects its unique characteristics and genetic makeup. Its classification system helps in understanding the evolutionary relationships and diversity within the plant kingdom.

side_effects: Tecomella undulata, also known as the Western Australian Peacock Flower, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. However, its use can cause side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal issues in some individuals. The plant contains compounds that may interact with certain medications, including blood thinners and diabetes medications.
